content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers NNN and KKK.

Let F(X)=(X mod K)×((N−X) mod K)F(X) = (X \bmod K)\times ((N-X)\bmod K)F(X)=(XmodK)×((N−X)modK), where  mod \bmodmod denotes the modulo operator.

Find an integer XXX such that the value of F(X)F(X)F(X) is the **maximum** over all 0≤X≤N0\le X \le N0≤X≤N.

If there are multiple answers, you may print any.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing two integers NNN and KKK.


Output Format
For each testcase, print a single integer XXX (0≤X≤N)(0\leq X\leq N)(0≤X≤N) such that the value of F(X)F(X)F(X) is the maximum over all possible XXX from 000 to NNN.


If there are multiple answers, you may print any.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers N and K separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer X (0 ≤ X ≤ N) such that F(X) = (X mod K) × ((N‑X) mod K) is maximized. If multiple X give the same maximum, output any of them.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: F(X)=0F(X)=0F(X)=0 for all 0≤X≤50\leq X\leq 50≤X≤5.
Test case 222:

F(0)=(0 mod 7)×(4 mod 7)=0F(0) = (0\bmod 7)\times(4\bmod 7) = 0F(0)=(0mod7)×(4mod7)=0
F(1)=(1 mod 7)×(3 mod 7)=3F(1) = (1\bmod 7)\times(3\bmod 7) = 3F(1)=(1mod7)×(3mod7)=3
F(2)=(2 mod 7)×(2 mod 7)=4F(2) = (2\bmod 7)\times(2\bmod 7) = 4F(2)=(2mod7)×(2mod7)=4
F(3)=(3 mod 7)×(1 mod 7)=3F(3) = (3\bmod 7)\times(1\bmod 7) = 3F(3)=(3mod7)×(1mod7)=3
F(4)=(4 mod 7)×(0 mod 7)=0F(4) = (4\bmod 7)\times(0\bmod 7) = 0F(4)=(4mod7)×(0mod7)=0

So, X=2X=2X=2 is the only correct answer.
Test case 333:

F(X)=0F(X) = 0F(X)=0 for X∈{0,2,3,5,6,8}X\in\{0,2,3,5,6,8\}X∈{0,2,3,5,6,8}
F(X)=1F(X) = 1F(X)=1 for X∈{1,4,7}X\in\{1,4,7\}X∈{1,4,7}

So, 111, 444 and 777 are all correct answers.

solution.approach:
Let a = X mod K. Then (N‑X) mod K = (N mod K – a) mod K. Denote n = N mod K. For a feasible residue a (0 ≤ a ≤ min(K‑1, N)) we have
    F(a) = a * ((n‑a) mod K).
The function is a concave quadratic on each of the two intervals a ≤ n and a > n, therefore its maximum on an integer interval is attained either at an endpoint or at the integer(s) nearest to the vertex of the parabola. The vertices are at a = n/2 (for a ≤ n) and a = (K+n)/2 (for a > n). Consequently we only need to test a constant number of candidates:
    • 0 and limit = min(K‑1, N) (the two ends),
    • floor(n/2) and ceil(n/2) (if they lie in the range),
    • if limit > n, also floor((K+n)/2), ceil((K+n)/2) and n+1 (the first value after n).
For each candidate a we compute f = (a % K) * ((N‑a) % K) and keep the a with the largest f. Any X with X mod K = a works; the simplest choice is X = a (since a ≤ N). The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
Edge cases:
    • K = 1 ⇒ every term is 0, answer can be 0.
    • N < K ⇒ limit = N and the second interval does not exist; only the candidates around n/2 are needed.
    • N = 0 ⇒ only X = 0 is possible.
The product fits into 64‑bit signed integer (≤ (K‑1)^2 ≤ 10^18).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
